title: Life & Liberty
lead: ==Art 21==: life and personal liberty — from ‘procedure established by law’ to ==due process==, dignity and privacy. The ==golden triangle== of Arts 14, 19 and 21 after [[Maneka Gandhi v. UoI (1978)]].

items.title: Art 14 — Equality
bullets: Equality before law + equal protection of laws

items.title: Art 21 — Life & Liberty
bullets: Procedure established by law → ==due process of law==

blocks.text: [[Maneka Gandhi (1978)]]: issues concerning both 19 & 21 → ==Arts 14, 19 and 21== must all be satisfied; issues concerning only 21 → Arts 14 and 21 must be satisfied.

items.title: Criminal Justice
bullets: No delay in death-sentence execution: [[T.V. Vatheeswaran (1981)]]
Free & fair trial: [[DLF v. Central Coalfields (2009)]]

items.title: Socio-Economic
bullets: Education: [[Mohini Jain (1991)]] · Health: [[CERC v. UoI (1995)]] · Shelter: [[Chameli Singh (1996)]]
Right to sleep: [[Ramlila Maidan (2012)]] · Third gender: [[NALSA (2014)]]

items.title: Environment
bullets: Clean environment: [[MC Mehta (1986)]] · Pollution-free air: [[Subhash Kumar v. BH (1991)]]
Healthy environment: [[Research Foundation (2005)]] · Protection: [[Animal & Env Legal Defence Fund (1997)]]

items.title: New Interpretations (2026)
bullets: Safely walk on footpaths: [[Maniyar v. Ayyapan]]
Safely travel on highways: [[Phalodi Accident v. NHAI]]
Maternity leave for adoptive mothers: [[Hamsaanandini Nanduri v. UoI]]

items: [[Justice K.S. Puttaswamy v. UoI (2017)]]: privacy is an FR under ==Art 21 + 19== with a ==proportionality test==
==Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023==, operationalised by ==DPDP Rules, 2025==

items.title: Implications
bullets: Digital: ==right to be forgotten==, deemed consent, first-originator (IT Rules), personality rights
Security: facial recognition, 2024 Interception Rules, Sanchar Setu (2026)

blocks.text: In a democracy like India — diverse, vocal, and increasingly digitised — the right to privacy is not merely a legal shield but a philosophical cornerstone, demanding a new social contract grounded in trust, transparency, and respect for individuality.

items.title: Scope & Consent
bullets: Digital personal data in India + ==extraterritorial reach== (GDPR-like)
Consent: ==free, specific, informed, unconditional, unambiguous==; ==deemed consent== for state functions, employment, emergencies

items.title: Actors
bullets: ==Data Protection Board== — fully digital, 4-member; appeals to ==TDSAT==

items.title: Significant Data Fiduciaries
bullets: Annual ==DPIA== + independent audit + ==DPO== + algorithmic due diligence

items.title: Breach, Children, Transfers, Penalties
bullets: Breach: intimation ‘without delay’ + detailed report ==within 72 hours==
Penalties up to ==₹250 crore== (security safeguards), ₹200 cr (breach/child), ₹150 cr (SDF)

items.title: Key Issues
bullets: ==Wide state exemptions (s.17)== — no independent oversight; surveillance concerns post-Puttaswamy
==Weakened RTI==: amendment to s.8(1)(j) removes the public-interest balancing test
==Board independence==: members appointed and conditioned by the Centre — risk of executive capture
==MSME burden==: consent overhaul; 70%+ small advertisers must re-engineer ad-consent flows
Ambiguous cross-border ‘restricted list’; ==deemed consent's breadth== dilutes consent-first design
==Long runway==: core duties only from May 2027; limited to digital personal data — ==non-personal data unregulated==

items.title: Way Forward
bullets: ==Board independence==: fixed tenure, broad-based appointments, financial autonomy (SEBI/TRAI model)
Notify pending rules early; ==calibrated MSME support== (templates, sandboxes)
==Judicial/parliamentary oversight== of state processing per Puttaswamy proportionality
Interoperable ==Consent Manager ecosystem== (DigiLocker-like); multi-stakeholder review (==SARAL== intent); DPB capacity-building

head: Jurisdiction
Distinguishing feature
Lesson for India
rows: Independent supervisory authorities; ==turnover-linked fines (4%)==; strong data-subject rights
Strengthen DPB autonomy; proportionate, deterrent penalties
Judicially evolved erasure ([[Google Spain (2014)]]) balanced against free expression
==Opt-out== model for data sale; dedicated privacy agency

blocks.text: Delhi's winter smog — recurring GRAP curbs, school closures — reflects a deeper gap: India has ==no explicit constitutional right to a clean environment==; courts read it into existing provisions, making enforcement ==contingent and reactive==.

items: ==Constitutional foundations==: Art 21 (judicially expanded); ==Arts 48A & 51A(g)== (42nd CA, 1976) — DPSP + Fundamental Duty in tandem; Arts 32 & 226 (PIL); Art 14 (arbitrary clearances); ==Art 253== (treaty legislation, e.g. Stockholm 1972).

head: Case
Ruling
rows: Widened Art 21 — foundation for the environmental reading of ‘life’.
First recognised right to healthy environment (==Doon Valley mining==).
Pollution-free environment as FR; spawned the ‘Mehta cases’.
Read Arts 48A & 51A(g) with Art 21 — state obliged to protect environmental quality of life.
Introduced ==Precautionary Principle & Polluter Pays==; sustainable development.
==Public Trust Doctrine== — state holds natural resources in trust.
Scientific expertise & public participation in decisions.
Balanced dam development against environment & rehabilitation.
==Distinct right against adverse effects of climate change== — Arts 21 + 14.

items.title: Core Legal Principles
bullets: ==Precautionary principle== — act without full scientific certainty
==Polluter pays== — remediation costs on the polluter
==Public trust doctrine== — state as trustee (Kamal Nath)
==Absolute liability== — post-==Oleum Gas Leak==; stricter than English strict liability, no exceptions

items.title: Key Statutes
bullets: Water Act 1974 / Air Act 1981 — SPCB/CPCB powers
Forest Rights Act 2006; Biological Diversity Act 2002

items.title: Persistent Challenges
bullets: ==Weak enforcement==: under-resourced SPCBs, pressure dilutes compliance
==Judicial dependency==: every claim must anchor to another Part III right
Implementation gap: orders outrun administrative capacity

items.title: Best Practices Forward
bullets: Genuine public participation in EIA; community conservation (FRA model)
Enforce Polluter Pays/EPR; align with ==Glasgow, Kunming-Montreal 30-by-30==

sections.title: Euthanasia & the Right to Die with Dignity

head: Active euthanasia
Passive euthanasia
rows: ==Legal== subject to Common Cause / Harish Rana safeguards
Not protected — no ‘right to be killed’
Part of the ==right to die with dignity== under Art 21

head: Case
Ruling
rows: Art 21 does ==NOT include the right to die== — but distinguished euthanasia from suicide, opening the door.
==First recognition of passive euthanasia==; interim procedure — HC approval via 3-doctor committee.
==Right to die with dignity is a facet of Art 21==; validated ==Advance Medical Directives (living wills)==; two medical boards + magistrate oversight.
Simplified: ==notarised/gazetted attestation==; hospital + secondary board (5 yrs' experience); magistrate only informed.

==First-ever order implementing passive euthanasia== — withdrawal of CANH for a 32-year-old in a 13-year vegetative state; CANH is ‘medical treatment’; ==30-day cooling-off waived==; AIIMS palliative plan; Parliament urged to legislate.

items: ==Legal framework==: BNS abetment liability for active euthanasia; ==s.115 Mental Healthcare Act 2017== effectively decriminalises suicide attempts; ==no standalone euthanasia statute== — only SC guidelines (2023) and non-binding draft Health Ministry guidelines (2024).

==Practical issues==: multi-layered process slow outside metros — Harish Rana needed an ==extraordinary Art 32 petition==; shortage of qualified doctors for boards; families act informally, exposing doctors to legal risk.

==Ethical risks==: ==coercion== of the elderly/disabled amid costly care and weak palliative infrastructure — ‘disguised abandonment’; under-specified ==best-interests test== for incompetent patients.
==Institutional gap==: continuing legislative vacuum; weak state ombudsman mechanisms.

head: Jurisdiction
Model & key features
rows: First to codify ==active euthanasia==; 6 ‘due care’ criteria; post-facto regional committee review.
Passed Commons (2024); physician-assisted dying for adults with <6-month prognosis; awaiting Lords.
‘Medical aid in dying’ (==Oregon model==): doctor prescribes, patient self-administers; terminal illness only.
Voluntary assisted dying legalised 2019–22; terminal diagnosis, residency, ==≥2 physicians==.

items: ==Way forward==: a dedicated ==End-of-Life Care Act==; ==digital living-will registry== (Aadhaar-linked); ==Hospital Ethics Committees== authorising withdrawal within ~48 hours; tech-enabled oversight dashboards; short cooling-off + mandatory counselling; end-of-life ethics in medical curricula and expanded ==palliative care==.

blocks.text: India need not import an active-euthanasia model to honour its constitutional promise of dignity in life and dying. Refining the existing passive-euthanasia protocol can make end-of-life decisions accessible and humane, consistent with India's context, while guarding against misuse.
The true test of a just society is not how it protects the powerful, but how fiercely it guards the life and liberty of each person — especially against the might of the State.
